text.fullText: From: Marianne Edain <fh@whidbey.com> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/13/2026 5:24:41 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ You don't often get email from fh@whidbey.com. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, As a restoration ecologist with over 40 years' experience, I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. If it ain't broke, don't fix it. §401 has been working for half a century. Communities have relied on it to be able to do the necessary research to bring the full facts of any given proposal forward. Often those facts are well buried under project proponent obfuscation. Time is needed to ferret out those facts. The proposed short deadlines work against sufficient time to find the facts. The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship. We all need clean soil, clean air, and clean water. This proposal will further dirty water, to the detriment of all of us. Please have the grace to do the right thing and deny this rule amendment. Sincerely, Marianne Washington
